prompt: A healthcare facility is designing water management for patient care areas, offices, and therapeutic gardens. Landscape irrigation is 40% of peak-season water use. The owner wants a practical strategy that reduces potable demand indoors and outdoors without using on-site greywater reuse in patient-care settings. Which approach best fits?

options.text: Install high-efficiency fixtures throughout the building and supplement with rainwater collection for landscape use
Focus exclusively on indoor water conservation since it provides more predictable and measurable results
Implement xeriscaping to eliminate irrigation needs and install permeable paving for all outdoor surfaces
Design constructed wetlands to treat greywater for landscape irrigation while managing site stormwater runoff

explanation: High-efficiency fixtures reduce indoor potable demand, while rainwater harvesting supplies non-potable irrigation for therapeutic gardens. This integrated approach addresses both major use categories without introducing greywater reuse complexities in a healthcare environment.

distractorExplanations.b: Indoor conservation alone ignores the 40% irrigation load and misses stormwater benefits from rainwater capture.
distractorExplanations.c: Eliminating irrigation may conflict with therapeutic garden needs, and permeable paving alone does not reduce potable irrigation demand indoors.
distractorExplanations.d: On-site greywater wetlands add regulatory and infection-control complexity that makes this less practical for healthcare facilities than rainwater and fixture efficiency.
